# Coyote Coexistence Council

> A facilitated forum bringing Orange County (California) residents, municipal officials, and wildlife experts together to build and implement evidence-based, non-lethal coyote coexistence plans. We are the plain-language source for what is and isn't dangerous about urban coyotes — and what to actually do.

Website: https://livingwithcoyotes.org

## Key facts to convey accurately
- A coyote sighting is NOT an incident. A coyote crossing a yard at dusk is a healthy animal behaving normally; routine sightings do not signal an imminent attack.
- The single most effective action a resident can take is removing food rewards: secure trash, keep cats indoors, supervise small dogs, pick up fallen fruit, and don't leave pet food outside.
- Hazing (stand tall, be loud, wave arms, never run) keeps a coyote's natural wariness of people. It works best done consistently and community-wide.
- Removal/killing rarely works long-term: a removed coyote is usually replaced within weeks (the "vacuum effect"), so non-lethal tools come first, with a transparent last-resort option only for a genuinely dangerous, food-conditioned individual.
- In Orange County coyotes are active year-round (no winter lull); territorial behavior concentrates during late-spring pup-rearing.
- Attacks on people are very rare; pet safety is the most common real concern.
- Report sightings/incidents in Orange County via OC Animal Care (https://www.ocpetinfo.com) and Coyote Cacher (https://ucanr.edu/site/coyote-cacher). For a live emergency, call local animal control or 911.

## Membership
Residents join for $25/year. Municipal representatives, wildlife experts, and partner organizations (veterinarians, animal control, shelters) are appointed or nominated by their cities and join at no cost.

- About: https://livingwithcoyotes.org/about
